lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Hang out our banners on the outward walls;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The cry is still “They come:” our castle’s strength

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Will laugh a siege to scorn: here let them lie

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till famine and the ague eat them up:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Were they not forced with those that should be ours,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: We might have met them dareful, beard to beard,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And beat them backward home. A cry of women within. What is that noise?

lines.speaker: SEYTON
lines.text: It is the cry of women, my good lord.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I have almost forgot the taste of fears:

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The time has been, my senses would have cool’d

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To hear a night-shriek; and my fell of hair

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Would at a dismal treatise rouse and stir

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: As life were in’t: I have supp’d full with horrors;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Direness, familiar to my slaughterous thoughts,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Cannot once start me.

lines.speaker: SEYTON
lines.text: The queen, my lord, is dead.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: She should have died hereafter;

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: There would have been a time for such a word.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To-morrow, and to-morrow, and to-morrow,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Creeps in this petty pace from day to day

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To the last syllable of recorded time,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And all our yesterdays have lighted fools

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: The way to dusty death. Out, out, brief candle!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Life’s but a walking shadow, a poor player

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That struts and frets his hour upon the stage

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And then is heard no more: it is a tale

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Told by an idiot, full of sound and fury,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Signifying nothing.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Gracious my lord,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: I should report that which I say I saw,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: But know not how to do it.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Well, say, sir.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: As I did stand my watch upon the hill,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: I look’d toward Birnam, and anon, methought,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The wood began to move.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Liar and slave!

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Let me endure your wrath, if’t be not so:

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Within this three mile may you see it coming;

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: I say, a moving grove.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If thou speak’st false,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Upon the next tree shalt thou hang alive,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Till famine cling thee: if thy speech be sooth,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I care not if thou dost for me as much.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I pull in resolution, and begin

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: To doubt the equivocation of the fiend

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: That lies like truth: “Fear not, till Birnam wood

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Do come to Dunsinane:” and now a wood

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Comes toward Dunsinane. Arm, arm, and out!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: If this which he avouches does appear,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: There is nor flying hence nor tarrying here.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: I gin to be aweary of the sun,

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: And wish the estate o’ the world were now undone.

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: Ring the alarum-bell! Blow, wind! come, wrack!

lines.speaker: MACBETH
lines.text: At least we’ll die with harness on our back.

stage_directions.text: Enter Macbeth, Seyton, and Soldiers, with drum and colours.
Thou comest to use thy tongue; thy story quickly.

glosses.definition: battle flags displayed defiantly on the castle's outer walls
mock any siege; defy it
fever — starvation and sickness will destroy the besiegers
Macbeth's most chilling self-diagnosis: I have had my fill of horrors — they cannot move me anymore

glosses.phrase: Direness, familiar to my slaughterous thoughts
glosses.definition: dreadful things have become familiar to my murderous mind

glosses.phrase: The queen, my lord, is dead
glosses.definition: Seyton's terse announcement — Lady Macbeth's death delivered as a bureaucratic report
she should have died some time later — Macbeth's flat, disordered response; the phrase is deliberately unanchored
a proper occasion for (announcing) such a thing
the play's nihilist anthem; time as mechanical repetition, drained of meaning
a small, trivial pace — life figured as a minor stride across time
the very end of all history
every past day
the extinction of death — reducing the body to dust
life as a short-burning candle — a classic emblem
life as a mere shadow that walks — insubstantial, mimic of reality
unskilled actor; pathetic performer

glosses.phrase: struts and frets his hour upon the stage
glosses.definition: swaggers and worries for his brief stage time — life as a bad theatrical performance
meaning nothing at all — the speech's most famous phrase; the end of meaning itself
Birnam wood advancing to Dunsinane — the prophecy beginning to fulfill
Macbeth refuses the messenger's report — still in denial
shrivel; waste you away (from starvation)
the devil's double-speaking — the Witches' prophecy was literal but misleading
falsehood that has the appearance of truth

glosses.phrase: wish the estate o’ the world were now undone
glosses.definition: wish the ordered state of the world undone — the Witches' 1.1 paradox completing itself
the bell that rings the call to arms
destruction; ruin
armor

annotations.title: 'The queen, my lord, is dead' — the flatness is the point
annotations.body:
Seyton delivers Lady Macbeth's death in five words. Macbeth's reply is almost as short: 'She should have died hereafter; / There would have been a time for such a word.' The couple who opened Act 1 writing to each other about glorious futures now communicates through a functionary's terse report and a husband's disordered shrug.

The flatness is the point. Macbeth does not weep; he notes. The woman who drove him to regicide, the wife whose hands he once could not look at, the mother of his unborn children (the 'babe that milks me' of 1.7) — dead, and his first response is a schedule complaint. She should have died later.

Shakespeare's refusal to give Macbeth any grief at this moment is not authorial coldness. It is authorial precision. The man has 'supp'd full with horrors' (L13, one line earlier). The direness his slaughterous thoughts have learned has cauterized the response to death itself. The tragedy's argument — that continued evil extinguishes feeling — has arrived at its strongest demonstration.

annotations.title: 'Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow': nihilism in its context
annotations.body:
The speech's nihilism is not abstract. It arrives in a specific context: Macbeth has just learned his wife is dead, and Shakespeare immediately follows her death with his most extended meditation on meaninglessness. The 'signifying nothing' is addressed to the life that has just ended.

The speech's philosophical content has been read as precocious existentialism (Macbeth as Shakespeare's Camus), as medieval contemptus mundi (life as shadow in the tradition of Thomas à Kempis), and as characterization (the nihilism of a man who has destroyed everything he cared about). All three readings are right simultaneously.

What makes the speech bearable, theatrically, is the precision of its grammar. 'To-morrow, and to-morrow, and to-morrow, / Creeps in this petty pace from day to day / To the last syllable of recorded time.' The future and the past collapse into one mechanical process. The iambic pentameter ticks like a clock. Time is not the enemy of meaning here; time is the absence of meaning, revealed.

The speech is where Macbeth's tragedy concentrates. He has killed Duncan for a crown, lost his wife, killed innocents, and arrived at the conclusion that it was all sound and fury. Shakespeare does not correct him. The play's conclusion — Malcolm's restoration, the healing of the state — is given to others. Macbeth's voice, in his last major speech, finds only the void.
sources: G. Wilson Knight, *The Wheel of Fire* (1930)

annotations.title: 'The equivocation of the fiend'
annotations.body:
Told that Birnam wood is moving, Macbeth names his defeat: 'I pull in resolution, and begin / To doubt the equivocation of the fiend, / That lies like truth.' The doctrine the Porter associated with Henry Garnet in 2.3 — swearing truly while reserving mental truths — has been applied to him by the Sisters. His own 'serpent under the flower' method has been turned.

The line closes a structural arc. The play's first great paradox ('Fair is foul') was announced by the Witches in 1.1. Its second ('Look like the innocent flower, / But be the serpent under't') was delivered by Lady Macbeth in 1.5. Now, at the end, Macbeth is the victim of the same technique he helped promulgate. The political allegory (Garnet, Gunpowder Plot, Jesuit equivocation) meets its ethical match: equivocation as a doctrine of power returns to destroy those who practiced it.

trials.prompt: Seyton's 'The queen, my lord, is dead' is followed by Macbeth's:
options: 'She should have died hereafter' — a flat schedule complaint; the tragic point is the absence of grief
Immediately after Lady Macbeth's death announcement — the meaninglessness is addressed to her life
Final phrase — the arrival point of the argument; the end of meaning itself

trials.prompt: Macbeth naming 'the equivocation of the fiend':
options: Closes an arc — the Witches have used on him the 'serpent under the flower' technique he helped promulgate

trials.prompt: 'Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player / That struts and frets his hour upon the stage' figures life as:
options: A bad theatrical performance — 'poor player' is an unskilled actor

trials.prompt: The speech has been read as:
options: Multiple coherent readings at once: existentialism, medieval contemptus mundi, and character — Macbeth's psychological end-point
